title: Should You Use Digital Planners or Paper Planners?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You want to get organized. You bought a planner. You used it for a week. Now it's sitting in your backpack, untouched. Maybe you tried Google Calendar. That didn't stick either. The problem isn't you. It's finding the right system for how your brain works.

The Case for Paper Planners

Writing by hand helps you remember. The physical act of writing engages your brain differently than typing. Studies show that handwriting notes leads to better recall than typing them.

Paper planners also eliminate distractions. You can't get a notification from a notebook. You can't fall into a TikTok rabbit hole with a pen. If you struggle with phone addiction, paper might be better.

And there's something satisfying about crossing off a task with a pen. That small dopamine hit motivates you to complete the next task.

Paper Planner Pros and Cons

Pros:
• Better memory retention
• No screen distractions
• Satisfying to use
• Works offline
• You can decorate it (if that motivates you)

Cons:
• Can't set reminders
• Can't share with study groups
• Easy to lose
• Hard to search past notes
• Takes longer to write

The Case for Digital Planners

Google Calendar, Notion, Todoist, Trello — these tools can do things paper can't. Set a reminder for tomorrow at 3 PM. Share your study schedule with your group project teammates. Search for 'homework' and see every assignment due this month.

Digital planners are also always with you (if you have your phone). You can add a task while waiting for the bus. You can check your schedule between classes. You never forget your planner at home.

Digital Planner Pros and Cons

Pros:
• Reminders and notifications
• Searchable
• Shareable with others
• Always on your phone
• Easy to edit and move tasks

Cons:
• Distracting (notifications, other apps)
• Lower memory retention
• Requires battery and internet
• Can feel impersonal

The Hybrid Approach (Best for Most Students)

Use both. Here's a system that works:
• Paper planner for daily to-do lists and due dates (writing them down helps you remember)
• Google Calendar for class schedules, appointments, and reminders (so you don't miss anything)
• Notion or Trello for long-term projects and group collaboration

At the start of each week, transfer due dates from your paper planner to Google Calendar. Set reminders for two days before the due date. Now you have the memory benefits of writing and the safety net of notifications.

Try Before You Commit

Don't buy an expensive planner or pay for a premium digital tool until you've tried it. Use a free notebook for a week. Use Google Calendar (free) for a week. See what sticks.

The best planner is the one you'll actually use. A cheap paper planner you open every day is better than a $50 fancy planner sitting on your shelf. A simple Google Calendar you check every morning is better than Notion with 15 empty databases.

Experiment. Find what works for your brain. Then stick with it.
